speaker: interviewer
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm going to tap you at fifteen minutes so that we'll have enough time for photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll be done in like two minutes.

words.text: be done in like two minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just so you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's one little detail I still want to mention from camp. But it wouldn't have more than two, three minutes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about seeing the British?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so the-- the British were there. And-- and that was liberation. That was the end of the torture of the last few years of-- of living an impossible life. And really a series of miracles that saved my life and saved me from not going to Auschwitz and being killed over there. Not only my life, but life of my children and grandchildren and great-grandchildren. So that's why it was to see-- to see the British was an unbelievable dream to-- to see these soldiers. And they told us, please, don't hold us back. We are pursuing the enemy. We have no time. And we stayed overnight in Mechelen because the bridges were blown up. We couldn't get into Brussels. And somebody took us in for a-- for a couple of days. We slept in somebody's house. Very nice people. And then we went by truck to Brussels. We-- we had to pay. But no-- there were no trains going, but there was a truck that charged a certain amount of money and took us back into Brussels. And that was the liberation. We came back to Brussels. We had no apartment. We had no place where to live. We went to-- my sister was still in hiding. We stayed by her until we got an apartment. But little by little, life became normal. We-- we got back into an apartment, and we got back our furniture. And we got to back into business, and within a year, life became more normal again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you a couple questions. What-- what had-- do you know what happened to Jack, the informer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that the Germans took him with them. When they retreated, they took him with them. I don't know if they killed him or if he was later killed. I don't know. But I do know from some other people what happened. The commander of Breendonk, his name was Major Schmitt, was condemned to death and executed in Belgium. The commander of our camp, Frank, got 12 years in jail. So did his assistant, Boden. They couldn't pinpoint on them killing anybody. They were brutal. I was a witness at Boden's trial. Not at the trial because I had left for the United States just at that time. But before the trial, I-- they called me as a witness, and they put it down that I think I saw him kicking a pregnant woman in the stomach and other things. And I guess that's why he got 12 years jail. The Van Kohl, the horse head that I said, the Belgian SS, the brutal, he was condemned to death and executed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were two Jewish couples from Breendonk which were very brutal. They were both condemned to death and executed. And that's about what I know from-- from these people. The other SS guards, I don't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you something else. Do you think-- could it ever happen again, the Holocaust, something like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust could happen again if we don't do something about it. That's my opinion. I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to prevent the Holocaust, we have to have a very strong Israel with-- with the most powerful weapons there are-- atomic weapons, any kind of weapons, mass destruction weapons to defend ourself. We should never give them up and never trust anybody. We should have them. We should have something in our hands to threaten the others. Because we can't trust anybody, including the United States. Germany was a very democratic country. Jews were all over at the helm in Germany. Before Hitler, there was a Jewish Chancellor. A Chancellor in Germany is like the President of the United States. The most powerful person, Walter Rathenau, was Jewish. All this changed in a short time when the Nazis came to power. On the contrary, all this worked into the hands of the anti-Semites because they said, look, it's true. The radicals of the sages of Zion are true. The Jews are all over. Even now the chancellor is a Jew. All the professors are Jewish. The doctors are Jewish. The lawyers are Jewish. They infiltrated everything. So that played into the hands of the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to go to pictures now? Mementos? OK Mr. Capell, do you want to introduce everyone here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on my right is my oldest grandson Saul. Very wonderful person. On my left is my darling bride of 48 years, Eva. Wonderful person. And then there's my son Kenny, another wonderful person.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're all wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wonderful daughter-in-law Marcie. And little Sam is the cutest little thing in the area. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So have you spoken much with them about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they were-- when my children were little, I-- I did speak about it. And then went they grew up, they-- they were not very happy. The reaction was not very happy that I spoke that much about the Holocaust, most of the time because somehow the subject always came up. But now, they all seem to be very interested. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they want to participate in exploring the Holocaust, whatever happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would any of you want to add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would just add that-- that it's true that we we grew up hearing many, many stories from my father. It seemed to be incessant, just about at every meal. But-- and it's true. When we got older, we sort of tired of-- of having the Holocaust introduced while we were eating pasta. And I must say for myself, I had a very difficult time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believing the story of how my father survived. Not that I didn't believe him. It just-- I had never heard anyone else who had survived through this list that was created by the Jewish agency. And it wasn't until my sister in Israel researched it and wrote an article on it, where she found these lists and it seemed to be very real that it became a believable and very, very true and meaningful story for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say that for-- for us, this is a very happy moment to be able to be here with some of our children and grandchildren. And we're very grateful that we made it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess, everyone say bye. [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say goodbye, Sam. Say goodbye. Say goodbye. Say something. [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're-- you're too shy? OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in Belgium in 1930. It represents my father and me as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what sort of circumstances was this taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a-- there was some kind of a fair in the-- in the city. My father took me there. And we put our heads in that some kind of a cardboard picture-- cardboard picture. And it was such a-- a fun picture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These are the authentic yellow stars that the Germans or that the Jews all over Europe and in Belgium to wear at all time when they are on the street. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This is the order from the German authorities to report to the assembly camp in Mechelen to be deported to Auschwitz. The official reason on the order is to report for work. There is a stamp on the right side on top which says delivered by the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: How they survived the war? I just kept it, this. And I must have found it in the hiding place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the authentic transport number that Jews had to wear around their neck the day of the transport. As you see, it says the number 43. On the bottom it says 27, which means 27th transport. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On the back of this number, is my name and also a saying that I was re-scheduled from the previous transport, which was the 26th, and my number was number 51. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the-- a copy of the authentic certificate from the International Red Cross in Geneva informing the Germans that my family and myself are on the exchange list for Palestinians, telling them the number of the list, and the particular number of the person to be exchanged. That certificate was a tool to save my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of the camp of Mechelen, from which 25,000 Belgian Jews were deported to their death. This picture was taken three years ago. And as you see, there is some work being done. It's being converted into apartments. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of our hiding place, where we were hiding most of the time. As you can see, there's a ground floor. And on the left is the door that the Germans broke in when they came to pick us up. We lived on the first floor, the second floor, on the ground floor. And the basement was occupied by the woman that hid us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was also taken three years ago. In front of the door, I'm standing with my sister. That's the sister that escaped through the roof.
